chunk_summary: This section discusses transitioning from vegetarian diet counseling to acute malnutrition protocols for wasted children, emphasizing screening, referral criteria, and feeding regimens tailored to cultural and physiological needs.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for diagnosing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children aged 6–59 months.
Learn the referral triggers and complications requiring facility-based care for SAM.
Explore culturally appropriate feeding strategies within therapeutic protocols for vegetarian and vegan families.
Recognize the importance of appetite testing in determining outpatient versus facility-based care.
key_facts: SAM is defined by weight-for-height < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm, or bilateral pitting edema.
Routine screening for wasting should include MUAC measurement and checking for bilateral pitting edema in children aged 6–59 months.
Facility-based care is required for SAM children with complications such as anorexia, hypoglycemia, severe dehydration, or altered sensorium.
Milk-based therapeutic feeds are standard for facility-based care; vegan substitutions require specialist oversight.
Outpatient follow-up combines protocol-led nutrition rehabilitation with culturally tailored family foods.
reference_quote: Operational guidance uses appetite testing as a discriminator; poor appetite often indicates complications and higher mortality risk.
document_key_topics: Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) diagnostic criteria and referral triggers
Screening and management protocols for wasted children
